topics.title: Prove that sqrt(2) is irrational
prerequisites: A rational number can be written as p/q where p and q are integers with no common factors
An irrational number cannot be written as a ratio of two integers
Even numbers are divisible by 2; odd numbers are not
If a number squared is even, the number itself must be even
learning_objectives: Understand why assuming rationality leads to an impossible conclusion
Recognize that the proof technique (contradiction) is widely applicable
common_misconceptions: sqrt(2) is approximately rational so it is close enough to rational
You can prove something is irrational by just computing many decimal places
Proof by contradiction is circular reasoning
socratic_path: What would it mean for sqrt(2) to be rational? Can you write that down as an equation?
If sqrt(2) = p/q in lowest terms and we square both sides, what do we get?
If 2q² = p², what does that tell you about whether p² is even or odd?
If p² is even, must p itself be even? Why?
If p is even, write p = 2k and substitute. What happens to q?

topics.title: Why should you always switch doors in the Monty Hall problem?
prerequisites: Basic probability: probability of an event = favorable outcomes / total outcomes
Conditional probability: the probability of A given that B has occurred
Three doors, one prize, two goats
learning_objectives: Understand how new information (a revealed door) changes conditional probabilities
Recognize that the host's knowledge matters — the host never reveals the prize
Calculate the switching vs staying probability correctly (2/3 vs 1/3)
common_misconceptions: After one door is revealed there are only two doors so it is 50/50
It does not matter whether you switch or stay
The host revealing a door gives you no information
socratic_path: When you first pick a door, what is the probability the prize is behind it?
What is the probability the prize is behind one of the OTHER two doors?
When Monty opens one of those other doors (always showing a goat), does the probability behind YOUR door change?
If your door still has 1/3 probability, and one of the other doors is now open (0%), where must the remaining 2/3 go?
Imagine playing this game 300 times — roughly how many times would switching win?

topics.title: What is a limit in calculus and why does it matter?
prerequisites: Graphs of functions can approach a value without reaching it
learning_objectives: Understand limits as the value a function approaches (not necessarily reaches)
Recognize that limits make sense of instantaneous rate of change
Connect limits to derivatives and the slope of a tangent line
common_misconceptions: The limit equals the function value at that point
Limits only matter when a function is undefined
You cannot divide by zero so calculus with infinitesimals is invalid
socratic_path: If a car travels 100 km in 2 hours, what is its average speed? But what was its speed at exactly 1:00:00 pm?
How would you find the slope of a curve at a single point — not between two points?
If you shrink the interval between two points on a curve smaller and smaller, what happens to the slope between them?
What does it mean for something to approach zero without actually reaching it?

topics.title: Are some infinities bigger than others?
prerequisites: The natural numbers go on forever: 1, 2, 3, 4...
The real numbers include all decimals including irrationals
A bijection (one-to-one correspondence) between two sets means they have the same size
learning_objectives: Understand that infinite sets can have different cardinalities
Recognize that the natural numbers and even numbers have the same cardinality (countable)
Appreciate Cantor's diagonal argument showing real numbers are uncountable
common_misconceptions: All infinities are the same size
There are more even numbers than there are natural numbers (there are the same amount)
Infinity plus one is bigger than infinity
socratic_path: Can you match every natural number (1,2,3...) with an even number (2,4,6...)? What does that tell you?
If we can pair them up perfectly, are there more natural numbers or more even numbers?
Now try to list ALL real numbers between 0 and 1 in a single infinite list. Can you?
Cantor showed you can always construct a real number NOT on any such list — what does that prove?

topics.title: Why does a² + b² = c² for right triangles?
prerequisites: Area of a square is side × side
Area of a triangle is (base × height) / 2
A right angle is exactly 90 degrees
learning_objectives: Understand at least one visual/geometric proof of the Pythagorean theorem
Recognize that the theorem is about areas of squares, not just side lengths
Apply the theorem to find unknown side lengths
common_misconceptions: The theorem only works for the specific 3-4-5 triangle
c is always the longest number in the formula
The theorem is just a formula to memorize, not something that can be proven
socratic_path: If you draw a square on each side of a right triangle, what are the areas of those squares?
Can you arrange four identical right triangles inside a larger square and see what area is left over?
If the big square has side (a+b), what is its area? What are the areas of the four triangles inside it?
After removing the four triangles, what shapes remain, and what must their combined area equal?
